lesson:
手 (hand) + 術 (technique, art). Surgery as a hand-art — the technique of the hands. 術 is the same kanji in 技術 (ぎじゅつ, technology/skill) and 芸術 (げいじゅつ, art). It always implies mastery of a craft.

手術を受ける (to undergo surgery — literally "receive" it) is the standard patient-side phrase. 手術する is what the doctor does. 手術室 (しゅじゅつしつ, operating room). 手術が成功した (the surgery was successful).

Pronunciation note: しゅじゅつ is a tongue-twister for many learners. The じゅ in the middle gets swallowed easily. Practice it slowly: shu-ju-tsu. In fast speech, native speakers sometimes blur it to something like しじつ, but you should aim for the full form.

The kanji tell you everything: 火 (fire) + 傷 (wound, injury). A fire-wound. A burn. But the reading やけど comes from the native Japanese verb 焼ける (やける, to burn/be burned) — やけど is the noun form of what happens when you get burned.

火傷をする (to get burned) — note that you "do" a burn to yourself, using する. 熱いお茶で火傷した (I burned myself with hot tea). It covers everything from a minor kitchen scalding to a serious injury.

There's an idiom worth knowing: 大火傷をする in a figurative sense means to suffer a major setback — to "get burned" in business, a relationship, or a risky venture. Same metaphor as English, same visceral punch.

課 (lesson, section, to assign) + 題 (topic, title, problem). An assigned topic — something handed to you that needs solving or completing. In school: homework, a class project, a research assignment. In the workplace: a pending task, an unsolved problem, an area for improvement.

The workplace sense is the one that gives 課題 its real weight. 今後の課題 (challenges going forward) is a phrase you'll hear at the end of every Japanese business presentation. It means "things we still need to figure out" — a formal way of acknowledging that the work isn't done. 課題を解決する (solve a problem/task). 大きな課題が残っている (a major challenge remains).

Compare with 宿題 (しゅくだい, homework), which is specifically schoolwork assigned to take home. 課題 is broader — any assigned challenge, in any context.

You already know 子供 (こども, child). 子 by itself is shorter, warmer, and more versatile. いい子 (a good kid). あの子 (that kid/that girl). 男の子 / 女の子 (boy/girl). It's affectionate — calling someone 子 implies smallness, youth, or endearment.

But 子's real power is as a suffix. It attaches to words to create names for living things, concepts, or even people. 子猫 (こねこ, kitten). 子犬 (こいぬ, puppy). The 子 means "young one." In personal names, it's the most common ending for women's names — 花子, 洋子, 京子 — though this trend peaked mid-century and is now less dominant among younger generations.

The kanji itself is one of the simplest: a picture of a baby with outstretched arms. The horizontal stroke is the arms, the vertical stroke is the body, the bottom hook is the swaddling cloth. It's been a baby for 3,000 years.

確 (certain, sure) + 認 (to recognize, acknowledge). To make something certain by recognizing it — verification. This is the word of Japanese conscientiousness.

You will hear 確認してください (please confirm/verify) in every professional setting: offices, airports, hospitals, restaurants. Japanese work culture runs on double-checking. Before the train departs, the conductor does 指差し確認 (しさしかくにん, point-and-confirm) — physically pointing at each signal and gauge while calling out its status. This system, invented by Japanese railways, has been adopted worldwide because it dramatically reduces human error.

予約の確認 (reservation confirmation). もう一度確認する (check one more time). 確認が取れた (confirmation has been obtained). The word is everywhere because the habit of verifying is everywhere.

実 (fruit, truth, reality) + 際 (occasion, boundary, actual). Reality at the boundary — what you find when you get close enough to see what's really there, past the assumptions.

実際 is the pivot word that flips expectations. Whatever came before it was theory, rumor, or assumption. Whatever comes after is what's actually true. 簡単そうだけど、実際は難しい (it looks easy, but in reality it's hard). 実際に会ってみたら、いい人だった (when I actually met them, they were nice).

It works both as a noun (実際のところ, in actuality) and as an adverb (実際に, actually, in practice). The に form is more common in conversation. You'll reach for this word whenever you need to contrast what people think with what's real — which, in any language, is surprisingly often.

In the 1980s, Japan was gripped by a buzzword: 国際化 (こくさいか, internationalization). The country was becoming an economic superpower and needed to engage with the world. Politicians said it. Businesspeople said it. Schools launched English programs in its name. Whether the project succeeded is still debated, but the word 国際 embedded itself permanently in the vocabulary.

国際 works like a prefix — it doesn't stand alone the way English "international" does. It bolts onto nouns: 国際空港 (international airport), 国際電話 (international phone call), 国際関係 (international relations). To make it a full adjective, add 的: 国際的な問題 (an international issue).

The kanji: 国 (country) + 際 (boundary, between). The same 際 from 実際 — there it means "at the edge of reality," here it means the space where countries meet. Borders are where the interesting things happen.

会 (to meet) + 議 (to discuss, deliberate). People meeting to discuss things. That's a meeting. The compound is formal and workplace-specific — you wouldn't use 会議 for getting together with friends.

会議中 (かいぎちゅう, in a meeting) is the Japanese equivalent of a closed door. It means "do not disturb." 会議に出る (attend a meeting). 会議を開く (hold a meeting — literally "open" one). 会議室 (かいぎしつ, meeting room/conference room).

Japanese meetings have a reputation for being long and consensus-driven. The concept of 根回し (ねまわし, behind-the-scenes groundwork) means that by the time the formal 会議 happens, the real decisions have often already been made through informal conversations. The meeting itself is where the agreement gets formalized and everyone nods together. Understanding this helps you understand why 会議 in Japan can feel ritualistic rather than deliberative.

After all the 根回し (behind-the-scenes groundwork) is done, someone still has to formally put the idea on the table. That's 提案. 会議で新しいプランを提案した (I proposed a new plan at the meeting). いい提案がある (I have a good suggestion).

It sits in a middle register: more formal than アイデア (a casual idea you toss out) but less weighty than 提言 (ていげん, a formal recommendation from an expert body). 提案 is what you do in a meeting — you present a worked-out suggestion for others to consider.

The kanji 案 has a lovely image buried in it: 木 (tree) over 安 (peace). Sitting peacefully under a tree — the place where ideas come to you. Newton had his apple tree; Japanese has it baked into the character for "plan." The same 案 appears in 案内 (あんない, guidance) and 答案 (とうあん, answer sheet).

事務 (business affairs, clerical work) + 所 (place). The place where business gets done. Not a corner office in a skyscraper — 事務所 is more modest than that. A law office. A talent agency. A local government office. The word implies a working space where paperwork and administrative tasks happen.

事務所に戻る (return to the office). 弁護士事務所 (べんごしじむしょ, law office). 芸能事務所 (げいのうじむしょ, talent agency) — in the Japanese entertainment industry, 事務所 is the standard word for the agency that manages celebrities.

Compare: オフィス (from English "office") tends to refer to modern corporate workspaces, especially in tech and international business. 事務所 is broader and more traditional — the default Japanese word for any working office.

教 (to teach) + 授 (to give, bestow, confer). Teaching as a bestowal — not just passing along information but conferring knowledge with authority. The word carries a weight that the casual English "professor" sometimes doesn't.

In Japanese academia, 教授 is a specific rank — the highest regular faculty position, above 准教授 (じゅんきょうじゅ, associate professor) and 助教 (じょきょう, assistant professor). You don't call every university teacher 教授 — that would be like calling every doctor a surgeon. When addressing a professor, the title comes after the name: 田中教授. Or simply 教授 by itself, the way English speakers might say "Professor."

As a する-verb, 教授する means "to teach" in a formal, somewhat archaic sense — to instruct with expertise. You're more likely to see this in writing than to hear it in conversation.

世 (generation, world, era) + 紀 (chronicle, era, record). A recorded era — a century. 21世紀 (にじゅういっせいき, the 21st century). Simple, clean, works the same way as English.

But 世紀 has a dramatic second life in the phrase 世紀の — "of the century," meaning once-in-a-lifetime, era-defining. 世紀の大発見 (discovery of the century). 世紀の大事件 (incident of the century). Japanese media loves this construction. Anything sufficiently dramatic gets the 世紀の stamp.

The kanji 紀 contains the thread radical 糸 — the idea of spinning out a thread of time, weaving the chronicle. 世 originally depicted three sets of ten (三十), meaning a generation — roughly thirty years. Three characters that tell the story of time passing and being recorded.

A mash-up of あっち (over there) and こっち (over here), smashed together with neither direction winning. There and here and there and here — all over the place. あちこち探した (I searched all over). あちこちに花が咲いている (flowers are blooming here and there).

The word has an aimless, scattered quality. It's not a systematic search or an organized tour — it's bouncing around from place to place without a fixed plan. 旅行であちこち回った (I went around various places on my trip). 体のあちこちが痛い (various parts of my body hurt) — not just one spot, but scattered all over.

You might also hear あちらこちら, the polite full form. Same meaning, just longer and more formal.

The polite version of そっち. Where そっち points casually toward the listener's vicinity, そちら does the same thing with a bow. そちらはどうですか (how are things on your end?). そちらに届きましたか (did it arrive there?).

The real reason to learn そちら is its use as a polite "you." Japanese avoids direct second-person pronouns in formal contexts — saying あなた to a customer or superior is awkward at best. そちら sidesteps the problem: そちらのお名前は？ (your name?) — literally "the name of that-direction" but understood as "your name." そちらでよろしいですか (is that all right with you?). It's indirect, respectful, and useful in any customer-facing situation.

The polite form of あっち — pointing toward something far from both speaker and listener. あちらのテーブルへどうぞ (please go to that table over there). In restaurants, shops, and hotels, あちら is the word staff use to direct you.

Like そちら, あちら can refer to a person — but with distance. あちらの方 (that person over there) is polite third-person reference. あちらは田中さんです (that (person) is Tanaka-san).

Together, こちら・そちら・あちら form the polite spatial trio — near me, near you, far from both. They're the versions you use in any situation where こっち・そっち・あっち would feel too rough. In a job interview, at a reception desk, in a formal email — always 〜ちら, never 〜っち.

もし is the word that opens hypotheticals — the Japanese "if" that flags what follows as uncertain or conditional. もし時間があれば、行きたい (if I have time, I'd like to go). もし明日雨だったら、中止です (if it rains tomorrow, it's cancelled).

Here's the key insight: もし is technically optional. Japanese has conditional forms built into verbs — 〜ば, 〜たら, 〜なら — and those already signal "if." Adding もし is like adding "just suppose" or "what if" in English — it emphasizes the hypothetical nature and signals the listener to shift into if-mode. Without もし, the sentence still works grammatically. With it, the conditional is foregrounded.

That makes もし a framing device more than a grammatical requirement. It's especially useful in longer sentences where the conditional clause might otherwise sneak up on the listener. Think of it as a courtesy: "heads up, we're entering hypothetical territory."

うるさい starts as "noisy" and escalates fast. 外がうるさい (it's noisy outside) — that's the basic meaning. But うるさい！ shouted at someone means "shut up!" — one of the rudest things you can say. And 彼は味にうるさい means "he's picky about flavor" — not loud at all, but fussy, particular, hard to please. Three meanings, one word, enormous tonal range.

The "picky" meaning is the one that surprises learners. あの人はルールにうるさい (that person is strict about rules). うちの母は掃除にうるさい (my mom is particular about cleaning). The logic: someone who is うるさい about something won't stop talking about it, won't let imperfections slide — they're annoyingly persistent in their standards. The noise metaphor became a metaphor for meticulousness.

Written in kanji as 五月蠅い — "May flies." Because flies buzzing around you in May are the original うるさい experience. This ateji (kanji chosen for meaning, not sound) is so perfect that it stuck.

Dictionaryうるさい
Negativeうるさくない
Pastうるさかった
Past negうるさくなかった
Adverbうるさく
て-formうるさくて

Japanese has three kanji all read かたい, and they split the concept of "hard" into three territories. 固い is hard in the sense of firm, solid, rigid — a 固い約束 (a firm promise), 固い握手 (a firm handshake), 固いパン (hard bread). 硬い is physically hard like metal or stone. 堅い is hard in the sense of reliable, steady, conservative — 堅い商売 (a solid business).

In practice, 固い is the default and covers most situations. The distinctions matter in writing but blend together in speech, since they all sound identical.

The figurative uses are where 固い gets interesting. 頭が固い (hardheaded, inflexible in thinking). 固い表情 (a stiff/tense expression). 固くなる (to stiffen up — from nervousness). A person who is 固い is formal, uptight, serious to a fault — the opposite of loose and relaxed.

Dictionaryかたい
Negativeかたくない
Pastかたかった
Past negかたくなかった
Adverbかたく
て-formかたくて

The kanji 深 is water (氵) on the left and a component on the right that suggests something penetrating downward. Water going deep. The physical meaning comes first: 深い川 (a deep river), 深い穴 (a deep hole), 深い森 (a deep forest).

But Japanese uses 深い for abstract depth just as aggressively as English does, sometimes more so. 深い意味 (a deep meaning). 関係が深い (the relationship is deep/close). 興味が深い (deeply interested). And one that doesn't translate directly: 霧が深い — fog is "deep" in Japanese, not "thick." You wade into fog the way you wade into water.

Time gets the 深い treatment too. 夜が深い (the night is deep — late at night). 深夜 (しんや, deep night — the small hours). Night isn't late; it's deep. You're far below the surface of the day.

Dictionaryふかい
Negativeふかくない
Pastふかかった
Past negふかくなかった
Adverbふかく
て-formふかくて

The kanji spell out something bizarre: 面 (face) + 白い (white). A white face. The origin story: in ancient Japanese court performances, the audience's faces would go pale — literally white — with amazement or delight when something captivating happened. The white face of astonishment became the word for the thing that caused it. Whether this etymology is precisely true is debated, but it's old enough to be essentially fossilized.

面白い covers "interesting" and "funny" without distinguishing between them the way English does. この本は面白い could mean the book is interesting or that it's amusing — or both. 面白い人 is an interesting/fun person. 面白くない means either "not interesting" or "I'm not amused" — and in the latter case, watch out, because it can carry an edge of displeasure.

Sound anchor: o-mo-shi-ro-i — five syllables, the longest common adjective in this list. The length itself is memorable. And the しろ in the middle is literally 白 (white) — the white-faced audience, frozen in fascination.

Dictionaryおもしろい
Negativeおもしろくない
Pastおもしろかった
Past negおもしろくなかった
Adverbおもしろく
て-formおもしろくて

If you already know 動く (うごく, to move — intransitive), this is its transitive partner. The pattern is clean: 動く = it moves on its own. 動かす = you make it move. That 〜かす ending is a productive causative suffix in Japanese — you'll see it in other pairs like 飛ぶ/飛ばす, 沸く/沸かす. Recognizing the pattern saves you from treating each pair as separate vocabulary.

What 動かす moves is surprisingly broad. Furniture, a car, your body — sure. But also hearts: 人の心を動かす (to move someone's heart). And entire organizations: 会社を動かす (to run a company — literally, to make it move). The word doesn't distinguish between physical and metaphorical motion. If you're the force making something change position or state, it's 動かす.

Particles: 動かす takes を — the thing you move is your direct object. 机を動かす (move the desk).

Potential が動かせる: が marks what can be moved. 一人でこの岩が動かせる？ (can you move this rock alone?).

Passive に動かされる: に marks who moved it. 彼のスピーチに動かされた (I was moved by his speech).

Causative に…を動かさせる: に marks the person, を marks the thing. 部下に荷物を動かさせる (make the subordinate move the luggage).

五段 Godan他動詞 — pair: 動く (うごく)

meaning: to break (down), be broken, fall apart
lesson:
The kanji 壊 has 土 (earth) on the left and a complex right side that originally depicted a garment being torn apart. Earth crumbling, fabric tearing — destruction from within. That's exactly what 壊れる describes: something breaks, falls apart, ceases to function — and nobody is named as the cause. The machine broke. The relationship fell apart. The plan collapsed. The thing just... stopped working.

Pair: 壊れる is intransitive — things break on their own. 壊す (こわす) is transitive — you break them. パソコンが壊れた (the computer broke) vs パソコンを壊した (I broke the computer). Japanese speakers instinctively reach for the intransitive version when they don't want to assign blame — which is most of the time. Saying 壊した puts someone at fault. Saying 壊れた lets the object take responsibility for its own demise.

壊れる goes beyond physical objects. 体が壊れる (one's health breaks down). 約束が壊れる is possible but rare — for promises, 破れる is more natural. For electronics and appliances, 壊れる is the default word. Your washing machine, your phone, your car — when they stop working, they 壊れた.

Particles: 壊れる takes が — the broken thing is the subject, not an object. 時計が壊れた (the watch broke).

Causative を／に壊れさせる: making or letting something break. を for coercive — 機械を壊れさせた (caused the machine to break). に leans permissive.

復 (return, repeat) + 習 (learn, practice) = going back over what you've learned. The word assumes you've already studied the material once — this is the return trip. Its natural opposite is 予習 (よしゅう, preparation/preview), where 予 means "before" — studying material before it's taught. In Japanese school culture, the pair is drilled into students early: 予習と復習をしなさい (do your prep work and your review).

As a する-noun, it pairs directly with する: 復習する (to review). You'll also see 復習をする with the を, which is equally natural. The thing you're reviewing takes を: 漢字を復習する.

If you're using Anki alongside this app, 復習 is literally what you're doing every time you open it. The word is your daily practice, named.

The kanji 眉 is a pictograph — one of the few body-part kanji where you can still see the original drawing. The top portion represents the eyebrow hairs arching over the 目 (eye) at the bottom. It's a face in miniature: brow over eye.

まゆ shows up in some vivid expressions. 眉をひそめる (to furrow one's brows — expressing displeasure or doubt). 眉唾 (まゆつば, literally "eyebrow spit" — something dubious, fishy). That last one comes from an old belief that wetting your eyebrows with spit would protect you from being deceived by foxes. The word survived long after the superstition died.

客 covers three English words that feel quite different — customer, guest, visitor — and this tells you something important about how Japanese thinks about the relationship. Whether you're buying something at a store, visiting someone's home, or attending an event, you're the 客: the person who has come from outside and is being received. The common thread isn't commerce or hospitality specifically — it's the host-guest dynamic.

The polite prefix お transforms it: お客さん (おきゃくさん) is how you'd actually refer to a customer or guest in most situations. Using bare 客 sounds blunt — fine in compounds like 客室 (きゃくしつ, guest room) or 乗客 (じょうきゃく, passenger), but when talking about actual people, the お and さん are practically mandatory.

The kanji itself: 宀 (roof) over 各 (each, every). Someone who comes under your roof — a person arriving from elsewhere. The "each" component hints at the individual nature of the visit: each guest is a separate event of receiving.

Three syllables for a desk: tsu-ku-e. The kanji 机 is 木 (tree/wood) with 几 (a small table) — wood shaped into a flat surface you work on. 几 by itself is an ancient character showing the side profile of a low table with two legs. Stack wood on that concept and you get 机.

机 vs テーブル: Both are flat surfaces, but they live in different rooms. 机 is where you study or work — it implies sitting down and doing something productive. A student's desk, an office desk. テーブル is where you eat, set things down, or gather around. Dining table, coffee table. If someone says 机に向かう (to face one's desk), they're about to work. Nobody says that about a テーブル.

The Japanese bath is not a shower. 風呂 means the tub itself, the act of soaking in it, and the entire bathing ritual — washing outside the tub first, then lowering yourself into hot water that the whole family will share. The tub isn't for getting clean (that happens at the washing area). The tub is for soaking. When someone says お風呂に入る, they mean the full experience.

The kanji are curious: 風 (wind) and 呂 (spine, backbone — but here used phonetically). The word's origins are debated. One theory traces it to the steam rooms of medieval Japan, where hot wind (風) was the medium. Another connects it to a specific type of steam bath structure. Either way, the kanji are ateji — chosen for sound, not meaning.

お風呂 with the polite お is standard in daily conversation. Bare 風呂 sounds slightly rough. The compound 風呂場 (ふろば) means the bathroom itself — the room where the tub lives.

One syllable, one kanji, one specific meaning: 湯 is hot water. Not warm water, not water in general — hot water. The kanji has 氵 (water radical) on the left and 昜 (rising sun/heat) on the right. Water plus heat. Elegant.

This distinction matters in Japanese more than it does in English. 水 (みず) is cold or room-temperature water. 湯 is water that's been heated. They're not the same substance in the Japanese mind. お湯を沸かす (boil water — literally "heat the hot water," which sounds redundant in English but isn't in Japanese: you're bringing 湯 into existence).

湯 connects to the entire culture of bathing and hot springs. 銭湯 (せんとう, public bath — "coin hot-water"). 温泉 uses different kanji but the concept is the same world. When you see 湯 in a place name — and you will, all over Japan — it marks a spot with hot springs.

ぬるい is the temperature nobody wants. Not hot, not cold — disappointingly in between. Your bath has gone ぬるい. Your coffee is ぬるい. In a culture that takes both hot baths and cold beer seriously, lukewarm is a failure state.

The word has a figurative edge too. ぬるい考え means soft, weak, half-hearted thinking — lacking the intensity the situation demands. A coach might call a team's effort ぬるい. It carries genuine contempt: you're neither one thing nor the other, and that's worse than being wrong.

Sound: Say nu-ru-i slowly. The ぬ has a heavy, flat quality — there's no sharpness to it, no energy. The word sounds like what it describes.

Dictionaryぬるい
Negativeぬるくない
Pastぬるかった
Past negぬるくなかった
Adverbぬるく
て-formぬるくて

A clean compound: 風邪 (かぜ, a cold) + 薬 (くすり, medicine) = cold medicine. Notice the く voices to ぐ at the join — this is rendaku, the voicing phenomenon that happens when two words fuse into a compound. くすり becomes ぐすり. You'll see rendaku everywhere once you start looking: 花火 (はなび, not はなひ), 青空 (あおぞら, not あおそら).

In Japan, 風邪薬 is available at any pharmacy (薬局, やっきょく) without a prescription. Brands like Pabron and Lulu are household names. Telling someone 風邪薬を飲んだ？ (did you take cold medicine?) is a standard piece of Japanese concern-showing.

靴 (くつ, shoes) + 下 (した, under/below) = the thing under your shoes. Socks are shoe-unders. Japanese names for clothing are often this literal: 靴下 tells you exactly where the garment goes.

The word matters practically because of the shoe-removal culture. In homes, schools, temples, many restaurants, and some workplaces, you take your shoes off at the entrance. Your 靴下 are suddenly on display. Showing up with holes in them is a minor social embarrassment that Japanese people are quietly vigilant about. The expression 靴下に穴が開いている (there's a hole in my socks) carries a small note of dread.

One of the oldest European loanwords in Japanese. Portuguese traders brought tobacco to Japan in the 1500s, and the Portuguese word tabaco came with it. It arrived centuries before Japan started using katakana for foreign words, so it stuck as hiragana — or sometimes the kanji 煙草 (literally "smoke grass"), though that's becoming old-fashioned.

In daily use, たばこ almost always means a cigarette rather than tobacco in general. たばこを吸う (to smoke a cigarette — literally "inhale tobacco"). The counter-phrase: たばこをやめる (to quit smoking). Japan's relationship with smoking has shifted dramatically — most public spaces are now non-smoking, and designated smoking areas (喫煙所, きつえんじょ) are increasingly small and isolated. But the word itself remains common.

Straight English: "ice coffee" rendered in katakana. Japan didn't just borrow the word — it arguably invented the concept. Iced coffee became widespread in Japanese kissaten (coffee houses) in the 1920s and 30s, decades before it caught on in the West. When you order アイスコーヒー in Japan, you typically get black iced coffee with a small pitcher of simple syrup and cream on the side.

In casual speech, this gets shortened to アイコ — a common Japanese abbreviation pattern where long katakana words get clipped to their first two mora. パソコン from パーソナルコンピューター, エアコン from エアコンディショナー. Same instinct.

Another Portuguese legacy, probably from tempero (seasoning) or temporas (the Catholic Ember Days, when meat was forbidden and fried fish was eaten instead). Portuguese missionaries in 16th-century Nagasaki brought their frying technique; the Japanese perfected it into something unrecognizable from its origins. Modern tempura — a whisper-light batter, barely there, shattering on contact — is entirely Japanese in execution.

The writing is interesting: 天 (heaven) + ぷら in hiragana. Sometimes you'll see the full kanji 天麩羅, but that's ateji — kanji forced onto a foreign word for their sounds, not their meanings. Most people just write 天ぷら or てんぷら.

Know that 天ぷら in a Japanese context doesn't mean "fried stuff" generically. It specifically means items dipped in a light egg-and-flour batter and deep-fried. Tonkatsu (breaded pork cutlet) and karaage (fried chicken) are not tempura — different technique, different word.

The kanji 肉 is a pictograph of a piece of meat — the inner strokes represent the grain or sinew of flesh. It doubled as the source of the 月-shaped radical you see in dozens of body-part kanji: 腕 (arm), 胸 (chest), 腰 (waist), 肩 (shoulder). That radical isn't actually "moon" in those characters — it's a simplified 肉. Whenever you see 月 on the left side of a kanji related to the body, think meat, not moon.

On its own, 肉 usually means meat for eating. 肉を焼く (grill meat). When someone in Japan says just 肉 without specifying the animal, they often default to either beef or pork depending on the region — beef in Kansai (Osaka area), pork in Kanto (Tokyo area). Regional identity runs deep, even in meat.

The kanji say 昨夜 — "previous night" — but the reading is ゆうべ, which comes from 夕べ (evening). This is one of those cases where kanji and reading come from completely different words that got married. The Chinese-derived reading さくや also exists and sounds more formal or literary. In conversation, ゆうべ is the natural choice.

ゆうべ covers roughly from dusk through the night. It's flexible: ゆうべ何食べた？ could mean dinner time or late at night, depending on context. Compare with 昨日の夜 (きのうのよる), which means the same thing but sounds slightly more specific — you're pointing at the nighttime portion of yesterday rather than just saying "last night" as a unit.

meaning: to be in fashion, be popular
lesson:
The kanji 流行 (りゅうこう) means "trend" or "fashion" as a noun. But read the same kanji as a verb — はやる — and it means something is currently in style, spreading, catching on. Diseases also はやる: インフルエンザが流行っている (the flu is going around). Fashion and epidemics use the same verb because both spread through populations. The kanji literally mean "flowing movement" — things moving through a crowd.

Pay attention to the tense. 流行っている (ongoing) is far more common than the dictionary form, because you're usually describing a current state: something is popular right now. 今、この歌が流行っている (this song is popular right now).

Particles: 流行る takes が — the popular thing is the subject. 新しいゲームが流行っている (a new game is trending).

Causative を／に流行らせる: making something popular. を marks the thing you spread. 新しいダンスを流行らせた (made a new dance popular).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryはやるが
Negativeはやらないが
Politeはやりますが
Polite negはやりませんが
Pastはやったが
Polite pastはやりましたが
て-formはやってが
Volitional
Polite vol
Passive
Causativeはやらせるを／に
Caus-passはやらせられるに
Potential
Imperative
Imp なさい
Cond ～ばはやればが
Cond ～たらはやったらが

This is a compound verb: 連れる (つれる, to lead/take along) in its て-form + くる (to come). The て+くる pattern means doing something toward the speaker — bringing rather than taking. Its mirror is 連れていく (to take someone away from here).

People only. 連れてくる is for bringing living things — people, pets, occasionally livestock. For objects, you use 持ってくる (もってくる) instead. 友達を連れてきた (I brought a friend). 犬を連れてきた (I brought the dog). But: 本を持ってきた (I brought a book) — not 連れて.

The conjugation follows くる's irregular pattern. The 連れて portion doesn't change — all the inflection happens on くる: 連れてくる → 連れてきた → 連れてこない → 連れてきます.

Particles: を marks the person being brought. 彼女を連れてくる (bring her along).

Passive に連れてこられる: に marks the bringer. 親に連れてこられた (I was brought by my parents).

Causative に…を連れてこさせる: making someone bring a person. 部下に客を連れてこさせた (had my subordinate bring the client).

Irregular (くる)他動詞

Negativeつれてこないを
Politeつれてきますを
Polite negつれてきませんを

Polite pastつれてきましたを

Volitionalつれてこようを

Caus-passつれてこさせられるに

You already know 方 read as ほう (direction, way). Read as かた, it's a polite word for "person" — the upgrade from 人 (ひと) when you need to be respectful. あの方 (that person — polite) vs あの人 (that person — neutral). In customer service, announcements, and formal speech, 方 replaces 人 almost everywhere.

お客様の中に、お医者様はいらっしゃいますか？ — that kind of formal announcement would never say 人. But in slightly less formal contexts: あの方はどなたですか (who is that person?). The choice between 人 and 方 is one of the first register decisions a learner has to make, and getting it right signals awareness of social distance.

The kanji 零 has 雨 (rain) on top and 令 (command, order) on the bottom. The original Chinese meaning was "a fine drizzle" — tiny droplets falling, almost nothing. From there it drifted to mean "fragmentary, falling to pieces," and eventually "nothing at all." Zero as the logical endpoint of diminishing.

In practice, you'll hear ゼロ (from English) at least as often as れい. The split: れい is used in formal contexts — phone numbers read officially, temperatures, mathematical notation. ゼロ is casual and emphatic — 可能性はゼロ (the probability is zero). Phone numbers in daily conversation often use ゼロ too. Both are correct; register determines the choice.

雲 is 雨 (rain) over 云 (an ancient character meaning "to say" — used here for its swirling, rising shape). Rain hasn't fallen yet — it's still gathered above, curling upward. A cloud is pre-rain.

Watch out: くも is also the word for spider (蜘蛛). Same sound, wildly different kanji, totally unrelated words. Context prevents confusion — 空にくもが出てきた is obviously clouds; 部屋にくもがいた is obviously a spider. But it's the kind of homophone that might trip you up in listening practice.

The compound 雲海 (うんかい, sea of clouds) describes the view from a mountain when clouds fill the valleys below like an ocean. It's one of those Japanese words that compresses a whole visual experience into two syllables.

The kanji 空 sits at a remarkable intersection. Read そら, it's the sky — the visible dome overhead. Read から, it means empty. Read くう in compounds, it means air, void, or sky. Three readings, three meanings, all circling the same idea: the vast open nothing above us.

The Buddhist concept of 空 (くう, emptiness/void) is philosophically foundational — the idea that all things lack inherent existence. But for vocabulary purposes, the reading you need now is そら: the sky you look up at. 空が青い (the sky is blue). 空を見上げる (to look up at the sky).

The connection between sky and emptiness is intuitive: look up and what's there? Nothing — and everything. The kanji captures both.

人 (people) + 気 (spirit, energy, feeling) = the energy of people, the crowd's enthusiasm — popularity. When something has 人気, people are drawn to it. The word works as a noun that modifies with の or がある: 人気のある店 or 人気がある店 (a popular restaurant).

人気がある vs 人気がない is a simple binary that comes up constantly — popular or not. このバンドは若者に人気がある (this band is popular with young people). Notice the に marking the group that finds it popular.

Reading trap: 人気 read as ひとけ means something completely different: signs of human presence. 人気のない道 (ひとけのないみち — a deserted road, a road with no sign of people). Same kanji, opposite feeling. Context makes it clear.

You've seen お兄さん (おにいさん) as the standard way to refer to someone else's older brother. Drop the お and you get 兄さん — still polite, but now it's your own brother, used when talking to him directly or referring to him in a context that doesn't need maximum formality.

The family-term system in Japanese has this layered structure: 兄 (あに) is the humblest form — you'd use it to describe your own brother to outsiders. 兄さん (にいさん) is what you might call him at home. お兄さん (おにいさん) is the full polite version — used for other people's brothers, or as an address term for any young man you don't know well.

In anime and manga, you'll hear にいさん or the more intimate にいちゃん constantly. Real families vary — some use お兄ちゃん, some use first names, some use 兄さん. The word tells you about the speaker's relationship and register.

The sister counterpart to 兄さん. Same structure: 姉 (あね, humble) → 姉さん (ねえさん, familiar-polite) → お姉さん (おねえさん, full polite).

ねえさん doubles as a casual address term for any youngish woman in certain contexts — calling a waitress in a traditional izakaya, addressing a slightly older woman you know casually. It's warm but can feel old-fashioned. The variant ねえちゃん is rougher and more familiar — a little brother calling his sister, or a man addressing a young woman informally (which can sound rude depending on context).

The kanji 姉 is 女 (woman) + 市 (market — used for sound here). Not much visual story to it, but the word itself is deeply embedded in Japanese family vocabulary.

平 (peace, flat) + 成 (become, achieve) = "achieving peace." This was the era name (年号, ねんごう) for Emperor Akihito's reign, from January 8, 1989 to April 30, 2019. Japan uses both the Western calendar and its own era system simultaneously, and official documents, government forms, and many daily contexts still use era years: 平成31年 = 2019.

The current era is 令和 (れいわ, since May 2019). Before 平成 was 昭和 (しょうわ, 1926-1989). Japanese people often identify generations by era — 平成生まれ (born in Heisei) carries cultural connotations the way "millennial" does in English. To convert: Heisei year + 1988 = Western year. So 平成10年 = 1998.

The kanji 毎 is underappreciated. Look at it: it's 母 (mother) inside a repeating frame. The original meaning was "each time, every instance" — the idea of a mother who produces offspring again and again, each one a repetition. From that came the modern prefix meaning "every": 毎日 (every day), 毎週 (every week), 毎年 (every year), and here, 毎月.

Two readings exist: まいつき (hybrid — Chinese まい + Japanese つき) and まいげつ (fully Chinese-derived). まいつき is more common in speech. The word floats in sentences without a particle: 毎月家賃を払う (I pay rent every month). 毎月の給料 (monthly salary) uses の to modify a noun.

The 毎 pattern is one of those rare corners of Japanese that's completely regular and predictable. Enjoy it while it lasts.

The tricky part isn't the meaning — it's the reading. 半日 can be はんにち or はんじつ. The first is standard in conversation. The second appears in formal or written contexts. What you will not hear is はんひ — the 日 takes its on-reading here, not its kun-reading.

半日 in practice means "a significant chunk of a day" rather than a precise 12 hours. 半日かかった (it took half a day) almost always carries a note of frustration — that was too long. 半日コース (half-day course) and 半日休み (half-day off) are real categories in Japanese scheduling, usually meaning either morning-only or afternoon-only. The kanji 半 itself is a visual: a vertical line cutting something in two. Half.

Here's where the reading matters more than usual. 半月 read as はんつき = half a month, about two weeks. 半月 read as はんげつ = a half moon, the crescent shape in the sky. Same kanji, completely different images. The time-word uses the native Japanese つき; the astronomical term uses the Chinese-derived げつ. If you get the reading wrong, you're talking about the sky when you meant the calendar.

半月前に引っ越した (I moved two weeks ago). あと半月で卒業だ (graduation is in half a month). In daily life, 半月 sits in a useful gap — it's what you say when 二週間 (two weeks) feels too precise and しばらく (a while) feels too vague.

meaning: now I see, now I understand
lesson:
This is the Japanese "aha." Someone explains something and it clicks — なるほど. It literally breaks down as なる (to become) + ほど (to the extent that) — something like "it has become to that extent" — but nobody parses it that way anymore. It's a fixed expression that means "I see, that makes sense, I understand now."

Be careful with frequency. なるほど is a genuine response — you're acknowledging that what the other person said has landed. Using it as filler (nodding and saying なるほど every few seconds without actually processing anything) is a well-known conversational sin in Japanese. Some people find it slightly condescending when overused — as if you're evaluating what they said and granting approval. Use it when you genuinely mean "oh, I get it now."

In professional settings, なるほど can sound too casual when speaking to superiors. Safer alternatives: そうですか or おっしゃる通りですね.
